verses.text: Uma visão geral das gerações entre Adão e os filhos de Noé (cp. **Gn 5.3-32** ).

O registro dos descendentes de Noé estabelece o lugar de Abraão na história. O pano de fundo étnico das setenta nações do mundo fornece o contexto para a história de Israel (cp. **Gn 10** ).\n\n

Ancestral dos heteus: Hebraico ancestral de Hete.

1:14 the Jebusite, &c.—At 1 Chr 1:14-17 the names are not those of individuals, but of people who all sprang from Canaan; and as several of them became extinct or were amalgamated with their brethren, their national appellations are given instead of the personal names of their ancestors.

1:15 The Hivite, and the Arkite, and the Sinite: Three of the seven Canaanite peoples descended from Canaan (1:13-16; cp. Gen 10:15-18). The Chronicler lists them to root post-exilic Israel's claim to the land in the table of nations — the same peoples whose dispossession Joshua records (Josh 24:11).

1:16 The Arvadite, and the Zemarite, and the Hamathite: The closing trio of Canaanite peoples stretches the genealogy north into Phoenicia. Arvad and Hamath remained major Aramean centers into the prophets' day (cp. Ezek 27:8, 11; Amos 6:2), framing Canaan's full geographic reach in the inherited promise.
1:17 Uz, and Hul, and Gether, and Meshech—or, "Mash"; these were the children of Aram, and grandsons of Shem (Gen 10:23).
1:18 Arphaxad begat Shelah—Cainan, the father's name, is omitted here. (See Luke 3:36).

1:19 The name of the one was Peleg - "Because in his days the inhabitants of the earth were divided according to their languages. And the name of his brother was Joktan, because in his days the years of men began to be shortened, on account of their iniquities." - T.

1:20 Joktan begat Almodad - "He divided and measured the earth by lines. Sheleph; he assigned rivers to be boundaries. Hazarmaveth; he prepared a place of snares to kill by the highways. Jerah; he built inns, and when any person came to eat and drink, he gave him deadly poison, and so took his property." - T. According to these traditions, the two first were geographers; the third, a public robber; and the fourth, an unprincipled innkeeper, who gave poison to his rich guests, that he might get their property.

1:21 Hadoram also, and Uzal, and Diklah: Three of the thirteen sons of Joktan (1:19-23), peopling southern Arabia. Uzal is linked to Sana (modern Yemen); Hadoram and Diklah are likewise Arabian. The Chronicler traces the Semitic peoples beyond Israel to show the wider human family from which Abraham was called (cp. Gen 10:26-29).

1:22 Ebal, and Abimael, and Sheba: Three more of Joktan's thirteen sons (cp. Gen 10:28). The name Ebal is given here as a variant of Obal in Genesis — minor textual variation common in ancient name-lists. Sheba founded a southern Arabian people later famed for its queen who visited Solomon (1 Kgs 10:1) and its merchants (Ezek 27:22).
1:23 And Ophir - "Whence gold is brought." And Havilah; "whence pearls are brought." - T.
O resumo dos descendentes de Sem termina com Abraão (cp. **Gn 11.10-26** ).

Seguindo o mesmo padrão do autor de Gênesis, o Cronista lista separadamente os descendentes de Abraão, além de Isaque, mencionando os filhos de Ismael ( **1.29-31** ; cp. **Gn 25.12-15** ) e os filhos de Quetura ( **1Cr 1.32-33** ; cp. **Gn 25.1-3** ) antes de detalhar os descendentes na linhagem familiar escolhida.\n\n

1:34–2:2 A genealogia de Isaque primeiro lista os descendentes de Esaú (1.35-54) e continua com Israel. A história de Edom é dada em três componentes: os filhos de Esaú (1.35-37), os descendentes de Seir (1.38-42) e os reis que governaram na terra de Edom (1.43-54). Este arranjo segue Gn 36.
